text: the “new fire” during the twilight wedding of the ages. Like that figure of the mythical diver, I found myself diving into a cosmic portal—one beyond the reefs, far below the waves, in the darkness and oblivion. There’s a wormhole, or serpent ladder, at the core of the world; it’s accessed in our brain’s core, with the assist of the heart mind. There’s an esoteric function of the psyche that involves a journey to the “afterlife,” in the “otherworld.” There’s a choreography of archetypal figures that occupy a universe centered on a human cosmic fractal. That otherworld is a liminal zone, with entry points to supernatural domains and encounters with great forces in the spirit. Here’s to the otherworld of the mystical heart, and the Perennial Philosophy and sacred geometry. Countless traditions the world over tell of heavens and hells, which are liminal worlds: partly psychological metaphors and partly biocosmic dimensions, or otherworlds , each connected to the middle , on earth. Tales of shamans from myriad cultures describe trips to the otherworlds, such as the folk legends of the Eskimos that tell of journeys to the otherworld on the aurora borealis. Much attention was paid to the otherworld by Carlos Castaneda, which he encountered in lucid dreams. Graham Hancock examines shamanic legends and ancient depictions of the otherworld and compares them to UFO narratives in his book Supernatural . The erudite Terence McKenna waxed poetic about the otherworlds, which he personally explored and recorded. In the philosophy of Siddhi Yoga (the yoga of mental training), visionary states are a given, although adepts are cautioned not to fixate on their otherworldly experiences. The otherworld is entered into through the imaginal realm. The soul encounters the otherworld by opening the heart mind, the feeling nature, which has a unique way of visualizing reality. But it’s submerged in an unconscious state for the most part; therefore, it’s akin to a lost continent, deep within. The point being that visions are not unprecedented and experiences with the otherworlds have been reported by every culture. Meanwhile, back at the cabana, the vision resumed: I was rising to the surface from the dark, watery abyss. All was a paradox: my trajectory was still downward, yet I was coming up out of the sea, which was a buoyant ascent—on the other side, descending feels like ascending. Even stranger, I could make out the world beyond the waves. Illuminated beyond the ocean’s surface was a landscape with a pyramid, as viewed from the sky. I’m rising out of the ocean and descending from the sky simultaneously! I know the otherworld is full of paradoxical situations, and this was certainly one of them. Now I was at ground level, looking at another style of pyramid. It was made of white stones, a step pyramid. I observed architectural features that looked like shapes found in nature: crystals, shells, feathers, and the like. I embraced the temple complex as an archetypal image, like the Heavenly City; its architectural motifs reminded me of cosmic ages and the regeneration of life forms. Seen another way, it was a design template (a temple) about the manifestation of conscious awareness. I later mused that the ruins of Tulum (and many other sacred sites) were an attempt to reproduce the many ineffable qualities of that eternal structure. I instinctively knew that I’d entered Xi Balba Be in the all-pervading galactic core, the Hu Nob Ku, City of White Stones, over whose moonlit walls drape beautiful, shady groves of incense-laden trees, above snow-white moonstones. It’s a very comfortable realm, with unique, symmetrical promenades shaped like the jaguar snout motif. I was aware of people and they were aware of me, but I didn’t see anyone. I was looking for Sanna as I wound through wide marble passages, enjoying the peaceful ambiance of shade trees in the moonlight. I had entered the House of the Dead and of Shadows, the Underworld, Xi Balba Be. Its white walls glowed like lotus petals, the fragrance of floral trees wafted on an imperceptible breeze. My soul had descended on the current of the Ida , drawn down by the full moon’s energy through a wormhole (in the earth—the serpent ladder) to the microcosmic orbit, the compressed core of an all-pervading singularity, the galactic core, via the root chakra. This is where the soul descends and reunites with loved ones and friends after life. It’s not at all hellish, but rather pleasant, like very bright moonlight on white marble terraces, under shady bowers of greenery. During the descent into the abyss, in the emptiness of oblivion, my attention split in two. One part continued descending, and the other ascended, to this: I was looking over a beautiful archipelago at a group of islands in a heavenly sparkling sea. Rising sunbeams splayed their fingers through clouds, framing the rich archipelago, in a glistening haze. I was gazing on the Central Sun, Alcyone, rising behind the isles of Paradise. In the mind’s eye, I saw paradise islands on a shimmering sea. This image coincided with a proprioceptive survey of the ventricles in the brain, made possible by the white flame piercing my heart. It’s love, unselfish love, that sears the heart, causing the soul to rise out of its pain like a phoenix; lifting the spirit all the way to the Paradise Isles in heaven’s crown, above and beyond the noonday sun. This is where her “symphony and song” informed me: “Sanna has the Central Beach of Paradise for her dance ceremonies, which are the best ever.” With these words, like solar flares, the sacred river rose in me and flowed from my eyes “like a mountain stream gushes by both sides of a rock.” Neither sobs nor weeping, but streams and currents of ecstasy and pain through my heart of stone, which now shone white-hot like the sun. At the same time, I was on the beach, breathing along with the waves at high tide, and I was struck by a conundrum:
